explicit_text: I was born in Mon-ya, Laos in 1946 and lived there until the war forced me to flee. Laos was my home and the place where my husband, Pa Ying Vue, fought as a soldier in the Secret War. Even after the war, some of my family, like my two brothers, remained in Laos. It was a difficult place during those times, especially with the conflict and loss we experienced.

explicit_text: My husband, Pa Ying Vue, was a soldier who fought in the Secret War in Laos alongside General Vang Pao and the CIA. This war deeply affected my family, as it was the reason we had to flee Laos and cross the Mekong River into Thailand. During this time, my husband led troops and was eventually killed in action. The Secret War shaped much of my journey and the hardships I faced as a widow and refugee.

explicit_text: The Mekong River was a major obstacle for my family and me when we fled Laos around the late 1970s. I remember the night we crossed it at about 11 o'clock, using bamboo, plastic bags, and rubber tubes to keep us safe and hidden. Crossing the Mekong River was terrifying but necessary to reach Thailand and escape the war. It marked the beginning of our long journey as refugees.

explicit_text: After crossing the Mekong River, my family and I entered Thailand, where we walked through the jungle for five days without food or shelter. We lived hidden in the jungles of Thailand for about three years to stay safe. Later, around 1977, the Thai government placed us in the Nom Kai refugee camp. Thailand was both a refuge and a place of hardship for us during those years.

explicit_text: Pa Ying Vue was my husband and a soldier who fought bravely in the Secret War in Laos. He led a troop of 12 men supporting the CIA and General Vang Pao before he was killed in action during the war. We were married for about a year before having our first child, and together we had five children. His death was a turning point for me, as I had to lead my family through the jungle and into refugee camps alone.

explicit_text: Around 1977, after living in the jungles of Thailand for three years, my family and I were placed in the Nom Kai refugee camp by the Thai government. The camp was crowded, with barbed wire fences, long food lines, and poor living conditions. We stayed there for about a year and a half while waiting for approval to immigrate to the United States. Life in the camp was difficult, but it was a step toward safety and a new life.

explicit_text: In 1979, my family and I were approved to resettle in the United States after being sponsored by a Hmong man named Mr. Thao. We first lived in Des Moines, Iowa, where the government helped us with basic needs. Later, we moved to Tulsa, Oklahoma, and then to La Crosse, Wisconsin, where I spent most of my life. The United States has been a place of safety and opportunity for me and my children, and I am grateful for the life we have here.

explicit_text: I am part of the Hmong people, an ethnic group deeply affected by the Secret War in Laos. Our community fought alongside the CIA and General Vang Pao, and many, like my husband, sacrificed their lives. After the war, many Hmong families, including mine, became refugees and eventually resettled in the United States with the help of fellow Hmong sponsors like Mr. Thao. Our shared history has shaped my identity and journey.

explicit_text: Now I live in Hudson, Wisconsin with my youngest son, Xai Vue, who is married with three children. I help watch my grandchildren here, which brings me joy. Hudson is where I have settled in my later years, surrounded by family and a peaceful environment.
